stewart_read: Emery states his bill clearly and precisely. Meg acknowledges and moves directly to credit score. The bill was captured and dropped — no flip, no emotional alignment-against-the-utility. The codex's capture-pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip'; Meg executed 'ask + acknowledge + move on.' This is the primary missed flip opportunity in the call. The number was on the table; the emotional leverage was not taken.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg was still in qualification mode and planned to anchor the bill emotionally later. Rejected — she never returned to the bill as an emotional lever at any point in the call.
coaching_implication: Play the 04:29 moment in the 1-on-1. Ask Meg: 'What did you do with that number?' Then walk through the objection_inversion move — the bill is the pain, not the solar payment. Suggested intent (not verbatim): 'A hundred and eighty-one dollars every month, forever, going up every year — that's what we're replacing.' The number was there. The flip was not.

stewart_read: Emery stated '$181.80.' Meg repeated back '$1.80 a month.' This is a concrete transcription/mishearing error. If this number enters the CRM or the closer's notes as $1.80, the proposal will be built on a figure two orders of magnitude below the actual bill. The closer walks in with a broken anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have been typing and miskeyed rather than genuinely mishearing. Either way, Emery did not correct her, which means the error may be in the record.
coaching_implication: Pull the CRM record for this call and verify what bill amount was logged. If $1.80 is in the system, correct it before the 02:30 appointment. In the 1-on-1, use this as a read-back discipline moment: always confirm the number back to the customer explicitly ('So $181.80 a month — did I get that right?'). One misheard digit can break the closer's entire proposal.

quote: so I'm a realtor, and I'm a investor, and I'm probably managing 30,000,000 in properties.
stewart_read: Emery is not a residential solar prospect who happens to own a three-flat. He is a commercial real estate investor with $30M in properties, a 60-unit resort in Belize, multiple Chicago apartment buildings, and $600K allocated for solar. The residential unit is the entry point, not the deal. Meg absorbed this disclosure without routing it — she noted 'equipment details' in her notes but did not flag the commercial opportunity for a different team or specialist.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg recognized the commercial scope was outside setter territory and intentionally deferred everything to the specialist. Partially supported — she does say 'I'll send the specialist out you guys can talk to and really explore all the options.' But she did not flag the commercial scale in her notes or route it to a commercial team, which is the gap.
coaching_implication: This is the call's highest-value missed moment. Ask the manager: does Ion have a commercial routing path? If yes, Meg needed to flag this in real time — not just note 'equipment details.' If no commercial path exists, the closer needs to know the residential appointment is an audition for a $600K+ relationship. Either way, the handoff note must reflect the commercial scope.

quote: I got 600,000 to put solar on the resort right now. So I'm trying to figure out what's gonna be my best plan.
stewart_read: Emery has capital allocated and is actively vendor-shopping for a commercial project. This is not a future possibility — it is a live buying signal with a stated budget. Meg's response ('For sure. For sure. So yeah. I mean, really, the first thing we need to do is get that usage.') pivots back to the residential bill collection without acknowledging the commercial signal at all. The codex's cross-sell routing logic applies: when a customer discloses a need beyond the residential scope, bridge and route.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have correctly assessed that Ion does not serve commercial/international projects and stayed in her lane. The international piece (Belize) is correctly DQ'd at 08:45. But the domestic apartment buildings and the resort's domestic components were not addressed.
coaching_implication: Two separate coaching points here: (1) The international DQ at 08:45 ('No. Right now, we're just in The States.') was handled correctly and cleanly — use it as a positive contrast. (2) The domestic commercial portfolio was not routed. Ask the manager: what is Ion's path for a customer with multiple domestic commercial properties? If there is one, Meg needed to surface it. If there isn't, the closer still needs to know the residential appointment is the foot in the door for a much larger conversation.

quote: well, what what what are you guys advertising?
stewart_read: This is Emery's second consecutive cost/structure question. Meg's answer ('We're advertising the bill swap program') is accurate but adds nothing to what she said at 05:24. She has no new angle. The codex's universal escape hatch — 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer' — was available here and would have been more effective than a repeated flat answer.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have been trying to hold the conversation open long enough to get the bill and book the appointment before bridging to the specialist. Partially supported — she does eventually bridge at 07:28. But three flat answers before the bridge cost credibility.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, walk through the escape hatch language from the codex. The rep doesn't need to know the answer — she needs to make not-knowing sound like a feature, not a limitation. Suggested intent: 'The reason I'm scheduling rather than quoting is that your situation — three-flat, multiple properties — deserves a specialist who can build the right model, not a ballpark from me.' That reframe was available at 05:24 and would have cut the three-touch cost/structure loop short.

quote: I drive a Tesla, so it gets a little bit
stewart_read: Emery mentions driving a Tesla while explaining why his bill might be higher — he's charging at home. This is a load-amplifier signal: EV charging at home increases electricity consumption and strengthens the bill-swap value proposition. Meg's response ('it's fine. Right. Charging it at home and running those bills up, I bet.') acknowledges it lightly but does not anchor it to the bill pain or the solar value. Note: Emery raised the Tesla in the context of his electric bill, not as a product expectation. No Tesla product reframe is warranted here per the codex ('only reframe when the customer raises it' as a product expectation).
coaching_implication: The EV-charging signal is a bill amplifier. Meg had the opening to say something like: 'So your bill is already $181 and you're charging a Tesla on top of that — that's exactly the kind of usage profile where solar makes the most sense.' She didn't. This is a secondary coaching point — not the priority — but worth noting as a missed anchor opportunity.

quote: Cool. K. Well, yeah, let's plan on, tomorrow, 02:30.
stewart_read: The button-up is incomplete. Meg confirms the time but does not deliver: (1) the one-hour block statement, (2) the no-driving / at-computer anchor, (3) the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake. She does mention a morning confirmation text, which partially covers the text-followup element. The appointment is booked but structurally soft — no verbal handshake, no computer/no-driving anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: The appointment is a phone call with a specialist, not an in-home visit — the 'at your computer' anchor still applies per the codex but the 'specialist come by' language Meg used at 07:30 suggests she may have framed this as an in-person visit, which would change the button-up requirements. If it's in-person, the computer anchor is moot but the verbal handshake still applies.
coaching_implication: Pull up the button_up checklist in the 1-on-1. Walk through what was hit (time confirmed, morning text promised) and what was missed (one-hour block, is-that-fair handshake, no-driving anchor). The 'is that fair?' line is the highest-value miss — it's the verbal commitment that predicts show rate. Two seconds to say; high cost to skip.

quote: Yeah. So the the reason is because when you're connected to the electric company, right, it your system is still tied to the electrical grid.
stewart_read: Emery pushed back on the hold at 01:38 ('This is my house. If I wanna put solar on it, I'm gonna put solar on it.'). Meg's response is to explain the technical reason for the hold — net metering, meter ownership, grid tie-in. This is scope creep under pressure: she's explaining solar infrastructure to justify a process step rather than simply holding the line on the process. The explanation didn't resolve Emery's irritation; it extended it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg was trying to show Emery she had a legitimate reason for the hold, not just stalling. Partially supported — transparency is better than silence. But the technical explanation was more than needed and invited further pushback.
coaching_implication: The simpler move under pushback: 'You're absolutely right — it's your house. I just want to make sure we set this up correctly for your unit specifically. Give me 60 seconds.' No technical explanation needed. The explanation at 01:43 is a scope-creep-under-pressure pattern worth naming in the 1-on-1.
